core_behavior.note: Model A specifies precise uptime targets and SPOF elimination; Model B focuses on error handling and algorithmic robustness; Model C uses vague qualitative statements about consistent performance and recovery, reflecting independent design choices for an undefined requirement.
core_behavior.note2: FC: all models fabricate specific reliability definitions despite the requirement providing none.

inputs.note: Model A includes infrastructure signals, health-check probes, and operator commands; Model B lists environmental and scheduled inputs; Model C includes external APIs and error reports, reflecting different assumptions about what inputs are relevant.

outputs.note: Model A specifies a structured /health endpoint with three states and detailed metrics at 1-minute granularity; Model B and C describe outputs at a higher level of abstraction without specific endpoint definitions or metric granularity.

assumptions.note: Model A has an empty assumptions array while Models B and C populate it with architectural and operational assumptions.

failure_modes.note: Model A lists failure scenarios as conditions; Model B lists failure modes as behavioral responses; Model C describes specific recovery actions per failure type, reflecting different structural interpretations of the field.

edge_cases.note: Model A has an empty edge_cases array while Models B and C populate it with scenarios such as unexpected inputs, peak load, and simultaneous component failures.

security_constraints.note: Model A has an empty security_constraints array while Models B and C specify authentication, encryption, secure coding practices, and vulnerability management.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies 99.9%/99.99% uptime and RTO/RPO values; Model B specifies 1-2 second response time without uptime figures; Model C specifies less than 0.1% downtime annually and sub-2-second response for 95th percentile, producing incompatible numeric thresholds across models.
performance_constraints.note2: FC: no model acknowledges that no SLA target was specified in the requirement; all models fabricate specific values.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 4 of the 8 fields, reflecting that the undefined requirement caused models to independently introduce incompatible design choices across core behavior, inputs, outputs, and failure modes.
